Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Computing platform"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Computing+platform%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Computing+platform%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Computing+platform%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Computing+platform%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Computing+platform%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Computing+platform%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Environment in which a piece of software is executed

A **computing platform**, **digital platform**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) or **software platform** is the infrastructure on which [software](/wiki/Software "Software") is executed. While the individual components of a computing platform may be obfuscated under layers of [abstraction](/wiki/Abstraction_(software_engineering) "Abstraction (software engineering)"), the *summation of the required components comprise the computing platform*.

Sometimes, the most relevant layer for a specific software is called a computing platform in itself to facilitate the communication, referring to the whole using only one of its attributes – i.e. using a [metonymy](/wiki/Metonymy "Metonymy").

For example, in a single computer system, this would be the computer's [architecture](/wiki/Computer_architecture "Computer architecture"), [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") (OS), and [runtime libraries](/wiki/Runtime_library "Runtime library").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In the case of an [application program](/wiki/Application_software "Application software") or a computer [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"), the most relevant layer is the operating system, so it can be called a platform itself (hence the term [cross-platform](/wiki/Cross-platform_software "Cross-platform software") for software that can be executed on multiple OSes, in this context).
In a multi-computer system, such as in the case of offloading processing, it would encompass both the host computer's [hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware"), [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") (OS), and [runtime libraries](/wiki/Runtime_library "Runtime library") along with other computers utilized for [processing](/wiki/Process_(computing) "Process (computing)") that are accessed via [application programming interfaces](/wiki/Application_programming_interface "Application programming interface") or a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"). As long as it is a required component for the program code to execute, it is part of the computing platform.

## Components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Computing_platform&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Components")]

Platforms may also include:

* Hardware alone, in the case of small [embedded systems](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system"). Embedded systems can access hardware directly, without an OS; this is referred to as running on "[bare metal](/wiki/Bare_metal_(computing) "Bare metal (computing)")".
* [Device drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver") and [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware").
* A [browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") in the case of web-based software. The browser itself runs on a hardware+OS platform, but this is not relevant to software running within the browser.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* An application, such as a spreadsheet or word processor, which hosts software written in an application-specific [scripting language](/wiki/Scripting_language "Scripting language"), such as an [Excel macro](/wiki/Excel_macro "Excel macro"). This can be extended to writing fully-fledged applications with the [Microsoft Office](/wiki/Microsoft_Office "Microsoft Office") suite as a platform.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* [Software frameworks](/wiki/Software_framework "Software framework") that provide ready-made functionality.
* [Cloud computing](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") and [Platform as a Service](/wiki/Platform_as_a_Service "Platform as a Service"). Extending the idea of a software framework, these allow application developers to build software out of components that are hosted not by the developer, but by the provider, with internet communication linking them together.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The social networking sites [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") and [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") are also considered development platforms.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* A [application virtual machine](/wiki/Application_virtual_machine "Application virtual machine") (VM) such as the [Java virtual machine](/wiki/Java_virtual_machine "Java virtual machine") or [.NET CLR](/wiki/Common_Language_Runtime "Common Language Runtime"). Applications are compiled into a format similar to machine code, known as [bytecode](/wiki/Bytecode "Bytecode"), which is then executed by the VM.
* A [virtualized](/wiki/Virtualization "Virtualization") version of a complete system, including virtualized hardware, OS, software, and storage. These allow, for instance, a typical Windows program to run on what is physically a Mac.

Some architectures have multiple layers, with each layer acting as a platform for the one above it. In general, a component only has to be adapted to the layer immediately beneath it. For instance, a Java program has to be written to use the Java virtual machine (JVM) and associated libraries as a platform but does not have to be adapted to run on the Windows, Linux or Macintosh OS platforms. However, the JVM, the layer beneath the application, does have to be built separately for each OS.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Operating system examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Computing_platform&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Operating system examples")]

Further information: [List of operating systems](/wiki/List_of_operating_systems "List of operating systems")

### Desktop, laptop, server

## Hardware examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Computing_platform&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hardware examples")]

Further information: [Lists of computers](/wiki/Lists_of_computers "Lists of computers")

* [ARM architecture](/wiki/ARM_architecture_family "ARM architecture family") based devices
  + [Raspberry Pi](/wiki/Raspberry_Pi "Raspberry Pi") or [Gumstix](/wiki/Gumstix "Gumstix") full function miniature computers
  + ARM servers with [Unix-like](/wiki/Unix-like "Unix-like") systems such as [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux") or [BSD](/wiki/Berkeley_Software_Distribution "Berkeley Software Distribution") variants
  + [ChromeBooks](/wiki/Chromebook "Chromebook") from various manufacturers
* [IBM PC compatible](/wiki/IBM_PC_compatible "IBM PC compatible") systems
* [IBM System p](/wiki/IBM_System_p "IBM System p") and [IBM Power Systems](/wiki/IBM_Power_Systems "IBM Power Systems") computers
* IBM [z/Architecture](/wiki/Z/Architecture "Z/Architecture") mainframes
* [CP/M](/wiki/CP/M "CP/M") computers based on the [S-100 bus](/wiki/S-100_bus "S-100 bus"), maybe the earliest [microcomputer](/wiki/Microcomputer "Microcomputer") platform
* [Video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), any variety ([PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation"), [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox"), [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"))
  + [3DO Interactive Multiplayer](/wiki/3DO_Interactive_Multiplayer "3DO Interactive Multiplayer"), that was licensed to manufacturers
  + [Apple Pippin](/wiki/Apple_Pippin "Apple Pippin"), a multimedia player platform for video game console development
* [Supercomputer](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer") architectures
